body:
## Summary `--capture browser` in the two CLIs parses the Google Docs editor model into the **same abstract block structure** (paragraphs with `content[]` of text runs + image nodes, plus `list` / `quote` / `style` / `horizontalRule` flags), but the **HTML serializer diverges** — so `document.html` in the archive already differs before html→markdown runs. The markdown defects cascade from that. On simple documents the difference is invisible; on documents where a single bold/italic span wraps multiple lines (soft line breaks, `Shift+Enter`) and contains an inline image, the two outputs diverge significantly. ## Repro (public, synthetic — no private data) Create a brand-new public Google Doc with this exact structure (copy into the editor as-is): > **Bulleted list item (click "Bulleted list" in the toolbar, then type):** > > Line one of bold text. \[Shift+Enter\] > Line two of bold text. \[Shift+Enter\] > \[Enter to start a new line, Shift+Enter again\] > Line three of bold text. > > Now place the cursor between line two and line three, and **insert an image** via Insert → Image → Upload. Select the entire list item (all three lines + image) and apply **Bold** (`Ctrl/⌘+B`). Make the document public (\"Anyone with the link\"). Then, in an empty directory: ```bash # isolated JS npm install -g @link-assistant/web-capture web-capture \"$DOC_URL\" --archive zip --capture browser unzip -p data/web-capture/**/document.zip document.html > js.html npm uninstall -g @link-assistant/web-capture # isolated Rust cargo install web-capture web-capture \"$DOC_URL\" --archive zip --capture browser unzip -p data/web-capture/**/document.zip document.html > rust.html cargo uninstall web-capture diff js.html rust.html ``` ## Observed The two `document.html` files differ structurally around the bold multi-line run with inline image: **JS emits per-run `` elements, closed across `
`:** ```html
  • Line one of bold text.
    Line two of bold text.

    Line three of bold text.
``` **Rust emits a single `` wrapping the whole multi-line run, broken by `
` inside it:** ```html
  • Line one of bold text.
    Line two of bold text.

    Line three of bold text.
``` Two other consistent deltas visible in the HTML output: - **JS** emits `` with `width=\"…\" height=\"…\"` attributes taken from the editor model; **Rust** emits `` with no dimensions. - The two HTML files use **different pretty-printing**: JS uses 2-space indentation with inner content indented; Rust uses 4-space-ish indentation with `
` lines inside ``. That is purely whitespace, not structure, but it does make the files non-byte-identical even when the tag tree matches. ## Markdown cascade When the two HTMLs are converted to markdown (JS: turndown, Rust: html2md), the `` grouping difference produces the observable markdown defects: - **JS** output: `**Line one**\n**Line two**\n**Line three**` — every line ends with a clean bold pair. - **Rust** output: `**Line one\nLine two` … image … `Line three**` — bold markers open and close only at the boundaries of the outer ``, leaving middle lines unbolded in rendered markdown. On pages with many such runs, stray `**` lines appear where html2md lifted the closing `` to its own line. This matches what we see on internal test corpora where multi-line bold runs with inline images are common — the two CLIs diverge on those runs while agreeing perfectly on well-formed single-line bold spans (see issue #102 observations, where `--capture browser` outputs on the issue-90 reference doc were byte-identical — that doc has no multi-line bold runs). ## Root cause pointer Both CLIs share the algorithm; they disagree on **serialization of consecutive text runs with the same active marks across soft line breaks** inside a paragraph/list/blockquote. - JS: `js/src/gdocs.js` → `renderContentHtml` (around L1197) calls `renderMarkedHtml` (L1215) per `ContentNode`, which re-opens/closes `` for every run. A paragraph with three bold runs around an image produces three `` elements. - Rust: `rust/src/gdocs.rs` → `render_content_html` (L2292) also calls `render_marked_html` per node, but the upstream parser appears to combine consecutive bold runs (including those containing `\n`) into a single `ContentNode` with embedded newlines. `render_marked_html` at L2326 then emits one `A
B`. When such a combined run is split by an image, the parser emits two combined runs — hence the \"bold – image – bold\" pattern visible in Rust's HTML but \"bold – /bold – image – bold – /bold – image – bold\" in JS. So the fix is either: 1. **Converge on JS's convention**: the Rust parser should keep consecutive runs with a newline between them as separate `ContentNode`s (or the HTML renderer should split on `\n` and re-wrap each segment). This would make Rust's output look like JS's (per-line ``), and markdown would come out with clean per-line `**...**`. 2. **Converge on Rust's convention**: the JS parser should merge adjacent runs with matching marks into a single node with embedded `\n`, and both HTML renderers should emit one `` per merged run. Markdown converters would need to be taught to re-wrap `…
…` as `**line1**\n**line2**` rather than treating it as one long bold span across newlines (otherwise Rust's current markdown defect stays). Convention (1) is cleaner because it produces markdown that survives round-tripping in every renderer we tested. Convention (2) requires downstream fixes in both html→markdown libraries. ## Ask 1. **Pick a single serialization convention** for multi-line bold/italic/strike runs and the `` attribute set, and implement it identically in both `js/src/gdocs.js` and `rust/src/gdocs.rs`. The `document.html` inside the archive should be byte-identical (modulo whitespace) between the two CLIs for any given input document. 2. **Bring `` attributes in sync**: either both emit `width`/`height` (JS today) or neither does (Rust today). Downstream markdown and reader-mode viewers behave better with the dimensions preserved, so option A is preferable. 3. **Cross-CLI HTML parity test**: extend `gdocs_public_doc` tests (both JS and Rust) with an assertion that normalized `document.html` is identical across the two CLIs for the #90 reference document *plus* a new public fixture that exercises multi-line marked runs with inline images. The fixture should live in `docs/case-studies/` so it's version-controlled. 4. **If multiple editor-model extraction paths exist** (e.g. DOCS_modelChunk vs. rendered DOM fallback), keep both mechanisms available and make them selectable via a flag (`--gdocs-source model|dom|auto`, default `auto`). But sync the *rendering* from the parsed model across languages regardless of which extraction path was used. Based on reading the two `gdocs` modules, both CLIs currently use the DOCS_modelChunk path exclusively; this is a \"keep it that way, add fallbacks behind a flag if you need them\" recommendation, not a \"multiple paths exist today\" claim. 5. **Reference target**: the archived `document.html` should be as close as possible to what the Google Docs editor would itself output for a file → download → HTML of the same doc. That gives a stable, external ground truth both CLIs can be measured against. ## Environment - **JS:** `@link-assistant/web-capture@1.7.14` (npm, installed in isolation) - **Rust:** `web-capture 0.3.6` (crates.io, installed in isolation) - Reproduced with each CLI installed alone (other uninstalled first) to rule out PATH shadowing. - macOS 24.6.0. ## Related - #90 — reference markdown round-trip document (closed; this is a follow-up exposing a class of bugs the reference doc doesn't cover). - #102 — `--capture api` fidelity defects (separate issue; `--capture browser` is significantly better but still diverges as shown above).
title: --capture browser: JS and Rust emit different HTML from the same editor model (strong/br grouping), cascading into different markdown
